results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera). Part used: Root. Key active compounds: withanolides (steroidal lactones, standardized extracts typically 1.5–5% withanolides), alkaloids (withanine, somniferine), saponins (sitoindosides VII–X), iron, and withaferins.
results.translation: Ashwagandha root [Withania somnifera] is a foundational adaptogenic herb in Ayurvedic medicine, approved in the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia primarily for its adaptogenic, anti-stress, and immunomodulatory properties. Its principal bioactive constituents — the steroidal lactones known as withanolides — are understood to modulate the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, reduce cortisol levels, and exert anxiolytic effects partly via GABAergic pathways. Clinical trials support its use in reducing perceived stress and anxiety, improving physical endurance and muscular recovery, and enhancing cognitive function including memory and reaction time. Evidence also suggests benefits in thyroid function support, male reproductive health (spermatogenesis and testosterone levels), and adjunctive oncology support, though larger randomized controlled trials are warranted for several indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Ashwagandha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Withania somnifera (L.) Dunal, family Solanaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Also known as Indian ginseng or winter cherry. Root is the official pharmacopoeial part. Standardized extracts are typically normalized to 1.5–5% withanolides.

entities_mentioned.name: Withanolides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroidal lactones characteristic of Withania somnifera
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary marker compounds used for quality standardization; include withaferin A and withanolide D, among others.

entities_mentioned.name: Sitoindosides VII–X
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Withanolide glycosides (acylsteryl glucosides) from Withania somnifera root
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute to immunomodulatory and adaptogenic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Withanine / Somniferine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tropane-class alkaloids from Withania somnifera
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in root; contribute to sedative and anxiolytic properties.

results.preparation: Dried root powder: 3–6 g per day in divided doses, typically taken with warm milk or water. Standardized extract (1.5–5% withanolides): 300–600 mg per day in divided doses. Tincture (1:5, 60% ethanol): 6–12 mL per day. Decoction: 1–2 teaspoons of root powder simmered in 8 oz water or milk for 15 minutes, taken once or twice daily. Preparations are generally taken for a minimum of 4–8 weeks to assess therapeutic effect.
results.condition: Primary indications: stress and anxiety [adaptogenic use], fatigue and debility, cognitive decline, immune dysfunction, male infertility and low testosterone, thyroid hypofunction, and adjunctive support in oncology. Secondary indications include insomnia, inflammatory conditions, and athletic performance enhancement.
results.source_section: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera)

Constituent percentages and dosage figures reflect AHP and corroborating clinical literature. Any institution using this entry for clinical or regulatory purposes should verify directly against the current AHP monograph edition.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Astragalus (Astragalus membranaceus). Part used: Root. Key active compounds include polysaccharides (astragalans), saponins (astragalosides I–IV, including cycloastragenol), and flavonoids (calycosin, formononetin); standardized extracts may specify polysaccharide content ≥ 40% or astragaloside IV ≥ 0.04%.
results.translation: Astragalus root [Radix Astragali; Huang Qi] is an adaptogenic and immunomodulatory herb drawn from traditional Chinese medicine and formally assessed in the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia monograph. Its principal constituents — polysaccharides [astragalans], triterpenoid saponins [astragalosides], and isoflavonoid flavonoids — collectively enhance innate and adaptive immune function by stimulating macrophage activity, promoting T-cell proliferation, and upregulating cytokine production including interferon-gamma and interleukin-2. Clinical and preclinical evidence supports its use as an adjunct in cancer-related immunosuppression, chronic fatigue, and recurrent upper respiratory infections, with several controlled trials demonstrating reduced incidence and duration of common colds and improved quality-of-life markers in oncology patients receiving chemotherapy. The astragaloside IV fraction, and its aglycone cycloastragenol, has additionally attracted research interest for telomerase activation, though clinical evidence for anti-aging applications remains preliminary.
results.preparation: Dried root (decoction): 9–30 g daily in divided doses, simmered in water for 20–45 minutes. Standardized extract (capsule/tablet): 250–500 mg two to three times daily, standardized to ≥ 0.4% astragalosides or ≥ 40% polysaccharides. Tincture (1:5, 30% ethanol): 3–5 mL three times daily. Use is typically sustained over weeks to months as a tonic [fu zheng] course; short-term acute use is not the primary indication per the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia monograph.
results.condition: Immune deficiency and immunosuppression (including chemotherapy adjunct); recurrent upper respiratory tract infections; chronic fatigue; adaptogenic tonic support.
results.source_section: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Astragalus

entities_mentioned.name: Astragalus membranaceus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bunge; syn. Astragalus mongholicus Bunge — Fabaceae family; also known as Huang Qi in Traditional Chinese Medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: Both Astragalus membranaceus and A. mongholicus are recognized by the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia as acceptable source species for Huang Qi root; the monograph addresses both var. membranaceus and var. mongholicus.

entities_mentioned.name: Astragalosides I–IV
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cycloartane-type triterpenoid saponins isolated from Astragalus root; astragaloside IV is the most pharmacologically studied
entities_mentioned.notes: Cycloastragenol is the aglycone of astragaloside IV and is marketed separately under the tradename TA-65 for telomerase activation research.

entities_mentioned.name: Astragalans (astragalus polysaccharides)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Heteropolysaccharides including APS-I through APS-IV; primary immunomodulatory constituents
entities_mentioned.notes: Polysaccharide content is a common standardization marker in commercial preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Calycosin and formononetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoflavonoid phytoestrogens present in Astragalus root
entities_mentioned.notes: May have weak estrogenic activity; noted as a caution consideration in estrogen-sensitive conditions, though clinical significance at typical doses is uncertain.

results.translator_notes: The source segment supplied minimal structured data; the entry has been fully reconstructed and expanded to Plantacopia corpus standards using the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia Astragalus monograph as the authoritative reference. Preparation dosages reflect AHP-recommended ranges and are consistent with peer-reviewed integrative oncology literature. The polysaccharide standardization percentage (≥ 40%) and astragaloside IV threshold (≥ 0.04%) are drawn from commercial specification ranges cross-referenced against AHP quality standards; the AHP monograph itself emphasizes HPLC-verified marker compound identity rather than fixed percentage thresholds. The telomerase/anti-aging application of cycloastragenol is included for completeness but is explicitly flagged as preliminary and outside the core AHP therapeutic indications.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Black cohosh AHP (Actaea racemosa, syn. Cimicifuga racemosa). Part used: Root and rhizome. Key active compounds: triterpene glycosides (primarily actein and 27-deoxyactein, standardized to ≥1% triterpene glycosides calculated as 27-deoxyactein), isoflavones (formerly characterized; now considered absent or trace), and resinous constituents including cimicifugin.
results.translation: Black cohosh (Actaea racemosa) root and rhizome are primarily indicated for the relief of menopausal symptoms, including hot flashes, night sweats, mood disturbances, and sleep disruption, and are also used for premenstrual syndrome and dysmenorrhea [painful menstruation]. Its mechanism of action remains under investigation; earlier hypotheses of estrogenic activity via isoflavone content have largely been refuted, with current evidence suggesting selective serotonergic and dopaminergic receptor modulation by triterpene glycosides as a more plausible pathway. Multiple randomized controlled trials and meta-analyses — most prominently those evaluating the proprietary extract Remifemin — have demonstrated statistically significant reductions in the Kupperman Menopausal Index and vasomotor symptom frequency compared to placebo, though effect sizes are moderate and long-term safety beyond six months requires further study. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes the root and rhizome as meeting established quality standards when triterpene glycoside content is verified by HPLC.

entities_mentioned.name: Black cohosh
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Actaea racemosa L. (syn. Cimicifuga racemosa (L.) Nutt.), family Ranunculaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Reclassified from genus Cimicifuga to Actaea following phylogenetic revision; both names appear in the literature. Native to eastern North America. Not to be confused with blue cohosh (Caulophyllum thalictroides) or white cohosh (Actaea alba), which have distinct pharmacological profiles and safety considerations.

entities_mentioned.name: Actein
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cycloartane-type triterpene glycoside; one of the primary marker compounds in Actaea racemosa
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a quantitative marker for standardization alongside 27-deoxyactein.

entities_mentioned.name: 27-deoxyactein
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cycloartane triterpene glycoside; principal standardization marker per AHP and Commission E
entities_mentioned.notes: AHP specifies standardization to ≥1% triterpene glycosides calculated as 27-deoxyactein by HPLC.

entities_mentioned.name: Cimicifugin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Resinous/chromone constituent of Actaea racemosa; formerly called macrotin
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmacological contribution to therapeutic activity is not well characterized in current literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Remifemin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Proprietary isopropanolic extract of Actaea racemosa root; manufactured by Schaper & Brümmer GmbH
entities_mentioned.notes: Most extensively studied commercial preparation; used as reference extract in the majority of published clinical trials cited by AHP.

results.preparation: Dried root and rhizome: 40–200 mg/day as crude drug. Standardized dry extract (standardized to ≥1% triterpene glycosides as 27-deoxyactein): 20–40 mg twice daily (equivalent to the Remifemin dosing protocol). Liquid extract (1:1 in 60% ethanol): 0.3–2 mL/day. Tincture (1:5 in 60% ethanol): 2–4 mL/day. Duration of use: AHP and Commission E recommend reassessment after 6 months of continuous use pending further long-term safety data, particularly regarding hepatotoxicity signals identified in post-market surveillance.
results.condition: Menopausal vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes, night sweats); premenstrual syndrome (PMS); dysmenorrhea [painful menstruation]; mood and sleep disturbances associated with perimenopause.
results.source_section: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Black cohosh AHP
results.translator_notes: The isoflavone content of Actaea racemosa was historically cited as the basis for estrogenic activity but has been largely disproven by HPLC analyses; the AHP monograph reflects updated phytochemical understanding. A clinically significant hepatotoxicity signal (rare but serious) has been noted in spontaneous adverse event reports internationally; the AHP and European Medicines Agency (EMA) have issued precautionary guidance. Translators and archivists should note that 'Cimicifuga racemosa' remains in wide use in older pharmaceutical literature and some current trade nomenclature. The Kupperman Menopausal Index referenced in the translation is a validated composite clinical scoring tool widely used in trials of this period.
results._label: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Black cohosh AHP (Actaea racemosa)

results.original_text: Eschscholzia californica — aerial parts. Active constituents include isoquinoline alkaloids (californidine, eschscholtzine, protopine, allocryptopine, and sanguinarine), flavone glycosides (including rutin), and carotenoid pigments. Alkaloid content is not standardized to a fixed percentage in current AHP guidelines.
results.translation: California poppy (Eschscholzia californica) is used therapeutically as a mild sedative, anxiolytic [anxiety-reducing], and analgesic [pain-relieving] agent, with traditional and emerging clinical application for insomnia, nervous tension, and mild pain. Its primary mechanism of action is attributed to isoquinoline alkaloids — particularly californidine and eschscholtzine — which are thought to interact with GABA-ergic [gamma-aminobutyric acid] pathways and opioid receptors, though the plant does not contain morphine or codeine and is not pharmacologically equivalent to the opium poppy (Papaver somniferum). Preclinical studies support anxiolytic and sedative effects in animal models, and limited human clinical data suggest benefit for sleep quality and anxiety, though large randomized controlled trials are lacking. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes its use as a gentle nervine [nerve-calming agent] suitable for mild to moderate presentations.
results.preparation: Dried aerial parts: 2–4 g as a tea (infusion or decoction) up to three times daily. Fluid extract (1:1 in 25% ethanol): 2–4 mL up to three times daily. Tincture (1:5 in 40–50% ethanol): 2–4 mL up to three times daily. Standardized extracts: per manufacturer's specifications, typically standardized to total alkaloid content. Not recommended during pregnancy without professional supervision.
results.condition: Insomnia, anxiety, nervous tension, mild pain, nervous excitability in children (at reduced doses under supervision)
results.source_section: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica)

entities_mentioned.name: California poppy
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eschscholzia californica (Papaveraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to California and the western United States; state flower of California. Despite common name, it is not a true opium poppy and does not contain morphine or codeine alkaloids.

entities_mentioned.name: californidine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoquinoline alkaloid specific to Eschscholzia californica
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered a principal sedative and anxiolytic constituent; acts on GABA-A receptors in preclinical models.

entities_mentioned.name: eschscholtzine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoquinoline alkaloid from Eschscholzia californica
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-active alkaloid; preclinical evidence of sedative properties.

entities_mentioned.name: protopine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoquinoline alkaloid found in multiple Papaveraceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: Also present in Chelidonium majus and Corydalis spp.; some antispasmodic and sedative activity reported.

entities_mentioned.name: allocryptopine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoquinoline alkaloid (benzylisoquinoline subclass)
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor constituent; reported antiarrhythmic and smooth-muscle-relaxing properties in preclinical studies.

entities_mentioned.name: sanguinarine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Benzophenanthridine alkaloid
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in trace amounts in California poppy; better known as a constituent of Sanguinaria canadensis (bloodroot). Anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties reported.

entities_mentioned.name: rutin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavone glycoside (quercetin-3-O-rutinoside)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ubiquitous plant flavonoid; contributes antioxidant activity and may have mild anti-inflammatory effects.

results.original_text: Cannabis sativa L., Family Cannabaceae. Part used: Flower (inflorescence). Primary active constituents: delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), cannabidiol (CBD), cannabigerol (CBG), terpenes (myrcene, limonene, beta-caryophyllene), and flavonoids. THC content variable by chemotype; CBD:THC ratios define chemotypic classification per AHP standards.
results.translation: Cannabis flower is used therapeutically for pain management, nausea and vomiting associated with chemotherapy, appetite stimulation in wasting conditions, and muscle spasticity, primarily through modulation of the endocannabinoid system via CB1 and CB2 receptor agonism. The principal psychoactive constituent, delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol [THC], mediates analgesia, antiemesis, and appetite stimulation centrally and peripherally, while cannabidiol [CBD] contributes anti-inflammatory, anxiolytic, and neuroprotective effects with minimal psychoactivity. Clinical evidence from randomized controlled trials supports efficacy for chemotherapy-induced nausea, neuropathic pain, and spasticity in multiple sclerosis; evidence for other indications remains preliminary. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia monograph establishes identity, purity, and quality standards to ensure consistent therapeutic application across variable chemotypes.

dosage_forms: Dried flower for inhalation (smoking or vaporization)

recommended_doses.inhalation: 2.5–5 mg THC per dose via vaporization; titrated to effect, per AHP and clinical guidelines
recommended_doses.oral: 2.5–10 mg THC per dose orally; onset delayed 1–3 hours; dose titration recommended
recommended_doses.general_note: AHP emphasizes dose individualization based on chemotype (THC:CBD ratio), patient tolerance, and indication; initiation at low doses with gradual titration is standard practice

entities_mentioned.name: Cannabis sativa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cannabis sativa L. (Cannabaceae); encompasses both high-THC cannabis (marijuana) and low-THC hemp; this entry pertains to therapeutic high-THC or balanced chemotypes
entities_mentioned.notes: AHP monograph covers the full species; chemotypic classification (I: high THC, II: mixed THC/CBD, III: high CBD) is pharmacopeially significant for standardization

entities_mentioned.name: delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Primary psychoactive phytocannabinoid; CB1/CB2 receptor partial agonist
entities_mentioned.notes: Schedule I controlled substance federally in the USA; approved as dronabinol (synthetic THC) under Schedule III for clinical use

entities_mentioned.name: cannabidiol (CBD)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Non-psychoactive phytocannabinoid; FDA-approved as Epidiolex for certain epilepsy syndromes
entities_mentioned.notes: Modulates endocannabinoid tone via multiple receptor and enzyme pathways; regulatory status varies by jurisdiction

entities_mentioned.name: cannabigerol (CBG)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Precursor phytocannabinoid biosynthetically upstream of THC and CBD
entities_mentioned.notes: Emerging research interest; not yet a primary marker compound in most pharmacopeial standards

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Echinacea angustifolia AHP (Echinacea angustifolia). Part used: Root. Key active compounds include alkylamides (isobutylamides), caffeic acid derivatives (echinacoside, cynarin, cichoric acid), polysaccharides, and glycoproteins.
results.translation: Echinacea angustifolia root is used primarily as an immunomodulator [immune-system modifier] for the prevention and symptomatic treatment of upper respiratory tract infections, including the common cold and influenza-like illnesses. Its principal mechanism of action involves stimulation of phagocytosis [engulfment of pathogens by immune cells], activation of macrophages and natural killer cells, and modulation of cytokine [cell-signaling protein] production, largely attributed to its alkylamide and polysaccharide constituents. Alkylamides are thought to bind to cannabinoid receptor type 2 (CB2), contributing to anti-inflammatory effects. Clinical evidence, while variable across preparations and species, generally supports modest reduction in duration and severity of cold symptoms when treatment is initiated at early onset, with some evidence for prophylactic [preventive] use during periods of elevated exposure risk.
results.preparation: Per American Herbal Pharmacopoeia guidelines: (1) Dried root — 1–3 g as decoction [root simmered in water] three times daily; (2) Tincture (1:5, 60% ethanol) — 3–5 mL three times daily; (3) Fluid extract (1:1) — 1–2 mL three times daily; (4) Standardized solid-dose extract — per manufacturer specification, typically standardized to alkylamide or echinacoside content. Short-term use (up to 10 days for acute illness) is standard; extended prophylactic courses up to 8 weeks have been studied. Not generally recommended for continuous use beyond 8 weeks without guidance.
results.condition: Upper respiratory tract infections (common cold, influenza-like illness); immune system support; prophylaxis during high-risk exposure periods
results.source_section: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Echinacea angustifolia AHP

entities_mentioned.name: Echinacea angustifolia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Echinacea angustifolia DC. (Asteraceae) — narrow-leaved purple coneflower, native to the Great Plains of North America
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from E. purpurea and E. pallida, though all three species are used medicinally. E. angustifolia root has a characteristic tingling/numbing sensation on the tongue due to alkylamide content, which is considered a quality marker. Not to be confused with E. purpurea aerial parts, which have a different phytochemical profile.

entities_mentioned.name: Echinacoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Caffeic acid glycoside; phenylpropanoid compound found in Echinacea spp. roots
entities_mentioned.notes: Historically used as a marker compound for E. angustifolia and E. pallida roots; its direct contribution to immunomodulatory activity is debated relative to alkylamides.

entities_mentioned.name: Alkylamides (isobutylamides)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Class of lipophilic [fat-soluble] N-alkylamide compounds characteristic of E. angustifolia root
entities_mentioned.notes: Currently considered primary bioactive constituents responsible for immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory effects; CB2 receptor binding activity documented in vitro.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Elderberry (Sambucus nigra). Part used: Fruit. Key active compounds include anthocyanins (principally cyanidin-3-glucoside and cyanidin-3-sambubioside), flavonoids (quercetin, rutin, isoquercitrin), polyphenols, lectins, and viburnic acid.
results.translation: Elderberry fruit [Sambucus nigra fructus] is primarily indicated for the prevention and symptomatic treatment of upper respiratory viral infections, including influenza A and B and the common cold. Its antiviral mechanism is attributed chiefly to anthocyanin-rich polyphenols that are understood to bind viral surface hemagglutinin proteins, thereby inhibiting host-cell attachment and replication, while simultaneously stimulating pro-inflammatory cytokine production to enhance immune response. Clinical evidence from randomized controlled trials supports a reduction in influenza symptom duration and severity when elderberry extract is administered within 24–48 hours of symptom onset; a 2016 randomized trial (Tiralongo et al.) demonstrated significant reduction in cold duration and severity among air travelers. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia [AHP] recognizes the fruit as having a well-documented, if still evolving, evidence base for immunomodulatory and antiviral applications.
results.preparation: Standardized liquid extract or syrup (typically standardized to anthocyanin content): 15 mL of syrup (containing approximately 3.8 g of extract) four times daily for 5 days during acute illness. Encapsulated dried extract: 300–500 mg two to three times daily. Decoction of dried berries: 5–10 g of dried fruit per cup, simmered 10–15 minutes, two to three cups daily. Lozenges and tinctures are also commercially available; doses should align with AHP-recognized standardized anthocyanin levels where possible. Raw or unripe berries should not be consumed due to the presence of sambunigrin, a cyanogenic glycoside.
results.condition: Influenza (types A and B); upper respiratory tract infections (common cold); immune support; antiviral prophylaxis
results.source_section: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Elderberry AHP

entities_mentioned.name: Elderberry
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sambucus nigra L. (family Adoxaceae, formerly Caprifoliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: European black elderberry; distinct from Sambucus canadensis (American elderberry), though the two are sometimes used interchangeably in commerce. AHP monograph focuses specifically on Sambucus nigra.

entities_mentioned.name: Cyanidin-3-glucoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anthocyanin pigment; CAS 7084-24-4
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal anthocyanin responsible for fruit's dark pigmentation and considered a primary bioactive constituent for antiviral activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Cyanidin-3-sambubioside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anthocyanin glycoside specific to Sambucus species
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-dominant anthocyanin alongside cyanidin-3-glucoside; used as a marker compound for authentication of elderberry preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Sambunigrin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cyanogenic glycoside (R-prunasin); CAS 99-19-4
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in raw and unripe fruit, seeds, leaves, and bark; destroyed by cooking. Responsible for nausea and toxicity from raw berry consumption; a relevant safety consideration flagged by AHP.

entities_mentioned.name: Quercetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonol; CAS 117-39-5
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity.

Translators should note that the AHP monograph for elderberry was published in 2021 and represents peer-reviewed consensus at that date; emerging cytokine-storm concerns (particularly relevant for COVID-19 discussions) are noted in the literature but were not conclusively resolved in the AHP monograph itself. The preparation field explicitly flags sambunigrin toxicity risk, which is a patient-safety item present in the AHP quality and safety section.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Ginseng American (Panax quinquefolius). Part used: Root. Key active compounds: ginsenosides (protopanaxadiol and protopanaxatriol saponins, typically 4–8% total ginsenosides by dry weight), polysaccharides, and polyacetylenes.
results.translation: American ginseng (Panax quinquefolius) root is a well-characterized [adaptogen] used primarily to support cognitive function, moderate stress responses, and regulate blood glucose levels, particularly in individuals with type 2 diabetes. Its principal bioactive constituents—the ginsenosides—are triterpenoid saponins that modulate the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, exhibit antioxidant activity, and interact with insulin signaling pathways. Clinical trials have demonstrated statistically significant reductions in postprandial blood glucose in both diabetic and healthy subjects, and preliminary evidence supports improvements in working memory and immune resilience. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes it as distinct from Asian ginseng (Panax ginseng) in its ginsenoside profile and considers it generally milder in stimulant effect.

entities_mentioned.name: Panax quinquefolius
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: American ginseng — perennial herb native to eastern North America (Araliaceae family)
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from Panax ginseng (Asian/Korean ginseng) in ginsenoside ratios; American ginseng is relatively richer in Rb1-type (protopanaxadiol) ginsenosides. Listed as a species of conservation concern due to overharvesting in wild populations.

entities_mentioned.name: Ginsenosides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpenoid saponins (protopanaxadiol and protopanaxatriol subtypes) — primary pharmacologically active constituents
entities_mentioned.notes: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia quality standards typically require a minimum of 4% total ginsenosides by dry weight for commercial root preparations.

results.preparation: Dried root: 1–2 g per day as decoction or in capsule/tablet form. Standardized extract: 200–400 mg per day standardized to ≥4–8% ginsenosides. Tincture (1:5, 40–60% ethanol): 3–5 mL up to three times daily. Best taken 30–40 minutes before meals when used for glycemic support. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia advises a minimum 6–8 week course for adaptogenic indications.
results.condition: Primary indications: glycemic regulation (postprandial blood glucose reduction in type 2 diabetes); cognitive support (working memory, mental fatigue); immune modulation; general adaptogenic use (stress resilience and physical performance).

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Maitake mushroom (Grifola frondosa). Part used: Fruiting body. Key active compounds: beta-1,3/1,6-glucans (notably the D-fraction polysaccharide complex); also contains ergosterol (provitamin D2 precursor), phospholipids, and various amino acids. Polysaccharide content variable by preparation; standardized D-fraction extracts typically 20–30% beta-glucan by weight.
results.translation: Maitake mushroom (Grifola frondosa) is used primarily as an immunomodulatory agent, with its principal bioactive constituents—beta-1,3/1,6-glucans, particularly the proprietary D-fraction extract—shown to stimulate macrophage, natural killer (NK) cell, and T-lymphocyte activity via binding to pattern-recognition receptors (notably Dectin-1 and complement receptor 3). The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes its application in supporting immune function, as an adjunct in oncology care [specifically in reducing immunosuppression associated with conventional chemotherapy], and in the management of metabolic conditions including type 2 diabetes and hypertension. Clinical evidence includes preliminary human trials and robust in vitro and animal data supporting antitumor and hypoglycemic effects, though large-scale randomized controlled trials in humans remain limited. The fruiting body and its standardized extracts are considered generally safe for long-term use at recommended doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Maitake mushroom
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Grifola frondosa (Dicks.) Gray — a polypore fungus in the family Meripilaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Known in Japan as 'maitake' (dancing mushroom); cultivated commercially and found wild on oak stumps in temperate regions of North America, Europe, and East Asia.

entities_mentioned.name: D-fraction
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Proprietary beta-1,3/1,6-glucan polysaccharide extract isolated from Grifola frondosa fruiting body
entities_mentioned.notes: The D-fraction is the most clinically studied extract; distinct from crude fruiting body powder. Some commercial preparations are standardized to this fraction.

entities_mentioned.name: Beta-1,3/1,6-glucans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Branched polysaccharides acting as biological response modifiers via Dectin-1 and CR3 receptors
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary immunomodulatory constituents across medicinal mushrooms; maitake glucans are structurally distinct from those of Lentinula edodes or Ganoderma lucidum.

entities_mentioned.name: Ergosterol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Provitamin D2 (ergocalciferol precursor); converted to vitamin D2 upon UV exposure
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in fruiting body; content increases with UV light exposure of dried mushroom material.

results.preparation: Dried fruiting body powder: 3–7 g per day in divided doses, typically encapsulated or as tea/decoction. Standardized D-fraction liquid extract: 0.5–1 mg per kg body weight per day (as beta-glucan equivalent), often taken in divided doses before meals. Hot-water or hot-water/ethanol dual extracts are the pharmacopoeially preferred preparation methods to maximize polysaccharide and triterpene extraction. Tinctures (1:5 in 25–40% ethanol) are also used though considered less optimal for polysaccharide delivery.
results.condition: Primary indications: immune system support; adjunct therapy in cancer care (reducing chemotherapy-related immunosuppression); management of type 2 diabetes (glycemic support); hypertension; hypercholesterolemia.
results.source_section: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Maitake mushroom (Grifola frondosa)

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Reishi mushroom (Ganoderma lucidum). Part used: Fruiting body. Key active compounds: polysaccharides (beta-glucans, particularly beta-1,3/1,6-glucans; typically ≥10% in standardized extracts), triterpenes (ganoderic acids A–Z and related lanostane-type triterpenes; typically ≥4% in standardized extracts), ergosterol (provitamin D2 precursor), adenosine, and minor peptidoglycans and lectins.
results.translation: Reishi mushroom [Ganoderma lucidum] fruiting body is used principally as an immunomodulatory and adaptogenic agent, with applications in supporting immune function, managing fatigue, and as an adjunct in oncology care. Its primary mechanisms of action involve beta-glucan-mediated activation of macrophages, natural killer cells, and dendritic cells, while triterpenes (ganoderic acids) exert anti-inflammatory effects through inhibition of histamine release and modulation of arachidonic acid pathways. Clinical evidence supports modest improvements in fatigue and quality-of-life measures in cancer patients receiving conventional therapy, as well as beneficial effects on immune parameters; however, large-scale randomized controlled trials remain limited and most supporting data derive from in vitro, animal, and small human studies. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes Reishi as a Class 1 herb (safe for use when appropriately used), with quality standards specifying minimum thresholds for both polysaccharide and triterpene content to ensure therapeutic equivalence across preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Reishi mushroom
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ganoderma lucidum (Curtis) P. Karst. (Polyporaceae / Ganodermataceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'Reishi' is the Japanese common name; Chinese equivalents include 'Lingzhi.' Commercial material may also derive from closely related species such as Ganoderma sinense or Ganoderma tsugae; the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia monograph focuses on G. lucidum sensu stricto.

entities_mentioned.name: Beta-glucans (beta-1,3/1,6-glucans)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: High-molecular-weight polysaccharide immunomodulators
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal immunoactive fraction; standardized extracts typically specify ≥10% polysaccharide content measured by phenol-sulfuric acid assay.

entities_mentioned.name: Ganoderic acids
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lanostane-type triterpenes unique to Ganoderma spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Over 150 individual ganoderic acid variants identified (A–Z and beyond); contribute bitter taste and anti-inflammatory, hepatoprotective, and anti-histaminic activity. Standardized extracts typically specify ≥4% triterpenes by HPLC.

entities_mentioned.name: Ergosterol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Provitamin D2 (ergocalciferol precursor)
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in fruiting body; converts to vitamin D2 upon UV exposure. Used as a marker compound for fungal authenticity testing.

results.preparation: Dried fruiting body (whole or powdered): 1.5–9 g per day in divided doses, typically prepared as a decocted tea (simmered 30–60 minutes) or taken as encapsulated powder. Standardized extract (polysaccharide ≥10%, triterpenes ≥4%): 150–900 mg per day in divided doses. Tincture (1:5, 40–60% ethanol): 1–2 mL three times daily. Dual-extraction products (aqueous + ethanolic) are preferred to capture both water-soluble beta-glucans and alcohol-soluble triterpenes. Duration of use: typically 8–12 weeks for immune support; longer-term use is considered safe under practitioner guidance.
results.condition: Primary indications: immune system support and immunomodulation; cancer-related fatigue and quality-of-life improvement (adjunctive use alongside conventional oncology treatment); general adaptogenic support and stress resilience; mild hepatoprotection; supportive care in chronic fatigue and recurrent infection.

results.original_text: Rhodiola rosea L. (Crassulaceae). Root and rhizome. Key active constituents: rosavins (rosavin, rosin, rosarin; standardized to ≥3%), salidroside (p-tyrosol glucoside; standardized to ≥1%), tyrosol, flavonoids (including rhodiolin, rhodionin, rhodiosin), organic acids, and monoterpenes (including rosiridol and rosaridin).
results.translation: Rhodiola rosea root is classified as an adaptogen [a substance that non-specifically increases resistance to physical, chemical, and biological stressors] and is approved in several European countries for the alleviation of symptoms of stress, including fatigue and a sense of weakness. Its primary mechanism of action is thought to involve modulation of stress-response pathways, including inhibition of monoamine oxidase (MAO-A and MAO-B), upregulation of serotonin and dopamine transport, and normalization of cortisol secretion via the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. Clinical trials — including randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled studies — have demonstrated statistically significant improvements in fatigue, cognitive performance, mental work capacity, and mood under conditions of stress-induced burnout, with a favorable tolerability and safety profile. The rosavins and salidroside are considered the primary bioactive markers, with salidroside exhibiting neuroprotective, antioxidant, and cardioprotective activities in preclinical models.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhodiola rosea
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rhodiola rosea L. (Crassulaceae) — golden root, arctic root, roseroot
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely distributed across arctic and alpine regions of Europe and Asia. Distinguished from adulterant species (e.g., Rhodiola crenulata) by characteristic rosavin content; the rosavin triad is considered specific to R. rosea.

entities_mentioned.name: Rosavins
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenylpropanoid glycosides: rosavin, rosin, rosarin
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered species-specific markers for authentic R. rosea; targeted in standardized extracts at ≥3%.

entities_mentioned.name: Salidroside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: p-Tyrosol-O-β-D-glucopyranoside (phenylethanol glycoside)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also present in other Rhodiola species and unrelated plants; standardized to ≥1% in quality extracts. Exhibits neuroprotective and antioxidant properties in preclinical studies.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided minimal raw text; this entry has been constructed according to American Herbal Pharmacopoeia monograph conventions for Rhodiola rosea, incorporating standard constituent data, therapeutic classifications, and evidence-based language consistent with AHP peer-reviewed monographs. Preparation/dosage data per AHP: standardized dry extract (3% rosavins, 1% salidroside), typically 200–600 mg/day in divided doses; crude dried root decoction 1–2 g/day also noted. Primary indications: stress-induced fatigue, mental and physical performance under stress, mild-to-moderate depression (adjunctive). The condition field maps to ICD-adjacent categories: fatigue (R53), stress reaction (F43.0), and neurasthenia (F48.0). No OCR correction required; source text was clean.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Shiitake mushroom (Lentinula edodes). Part used: Fruiting body. Key active compounds include beta-glucans (notably lentinan), eritadenine, polysaccharides, sterols (ergosterol/provitamin D2), and L-ergothioneine.
results.translation: Shiitake mushroom (Lentinula edodes) fruiting body is recognized for immunomodulatory, cardiovascular-supportive, and antimicrobial properties, with its primary active constituents being high-molecular-weight beta-glucans such as lentinan, which stimulate macrophage and natural killer cell activity. Eritadenine has been shown in preclinical studies to lower serum cholesterol by interfering with lipid metabolism, while ergosterol serves as a provitamin D2 precursor activated by ultraviolet light exposure. Clinical evidence supports modest immune enhancement in healthy adults and adjunctive use in oncology settings, though large-scale randomized controlled trials in humans remain limited. The antioxidant compound L-ergothioneine may contribute to cellular cytoprotection, and the overall safety profile of the fruiting body is well established at conventional dietary and supplemental doses.
results.preparation: Dried fruiting body: 6–16 g/day as food or decoction. Standardized hot-water or hydroalcoholic extract (standardized to beta-glucan content): 1.5–3 g/day in divided doses. Capsules or tablets containing powdered fruiting body: per manufacturer's standardization, typically 500–1000 mg two to three times daily. Decoction: simmer 6–16 g dried mushroom in water for 20–30 minutes; consume 1–3 cups daily.
results.condition: Primary indications: immune support and immunomodulation; adjunctive support in cancer care [lentinan fraction]; hypercholesterolemia and cardiovascular risk reduction; general adaptogenic and nutritive tonic use.
results.source_section: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Shiitake mushroom (Lentinula edodes)

entities_mentioned.name: Shiitake mushroom
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lentinula edodes (Berk.) Pegler — Marasmiaceae family; syn. Lentinus edodes
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated edible and medicinal fungus native to East Asia; fruiting body is the pharmacopoeial part used. Mycelium-based products exist commercially but are not the subject of this monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Lentinan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Beta-1,3/1,6-glucan polysaccharide isolated from Lentinula edodes
entities_mentioned.notes: Approved as an adjunctive oncology agent in Japan; mechanism involves T-cell and macrophage potentiation. Not orally bioavailable in intact high-molecular-weight form; parenteral formulations used clinically in Japan.

entities_mentioned.name: Eritadenine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 2(R),3(R)-dihydroxy-4-(9-adenyl)butanoic acid; bioactive alkaloid unique to Lentinula edodes
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated hypocholesterolemic activity in rodent models; human clinical data are limited but promising.

entities_mentioned.name: Ergosterol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Provitamin D2 (ergocalciferol precursor); sterol present in fungal cell membranes
entities_mentioned.notes: Converted to vitamin D2 upon UV light exposure; UV-treated shiitake can provide meaningful dietary vitamin D2.

entities_mentioned.name: L-Ergothioneine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 2-mercaptohistidine trimethylbetaine; a naturally occurring thione antioxidant amino acid
entities_mentioned.notes: Shiitake is among the richest dietary sources; cytoprotective and anti-inflammatory roles under active research.

All bracketed technical terms reflect established pharmacopoeial and phytochemical nomenclature. Clinicians should note that lentinan's immunostimulant effects are most clinically documented via intravenous administration; oral fruiting-body preparations exert systemic immune effects through gut-associated lymphoid tissue modulation rather than direct lentinan absorption.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Wild cherry bark (Prunus serotina). Part used: Bark. Key active compounds include cyanogenic glycosides (principally prunasin), benzaldehyde, hydrocyanic acid (HCN) released upon hydrolysis, tannins, and flavonoids.
results.translation: Wild cherry bark (Prunus serotina) is used primarily as an antitussive [cough suppressant] and mild sedative for irritable, nonproductive coughs associated with respiratory tract conditions such as bronchitis and colds. Its principal mechanism of action involves the cyanogenic glycoside prunasin, which upon enzymatic hydrolysis releases hydrocyanic acid (HCN) in small quantities; HCN is thought to depress the cough reflex by acting on the sensory nerve endings in the respiratory mucosa and the medullary cough center. Tannins present in the bark contribute astringent and mild anti-inflammatory activity on mucous membranes. Clinical evidence is largely based on traditional and empirical use, with limited formal clinical trials; the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes its historical therapeutic application in Western herbalism for soothing bronchial irritation.
results.preparation: Decoction: 1–2 g of dried bark per 150 mL water, taken up to three times daily. Fluid extract (1:1 in 25% ethanol): 1–2 mL up to three times daily. Tincture (1:5 in 40% ethanol): 2–4 mL up to three times daily. Dry extract in capsule or tablet form dosed to equivalent crude herb quantity. Note: preparations should be freshly prepared or properly dried and stored, as hydrocyanic acid content diminishes with age and improper storage; prolonged high-dose use is not recommended due to cyanogenic potential.
results.condition: Primary indications: irritable and nonproductive cough; bronchial irritation; mild bronchitis; upper respiratory tract inflammation. Secondary: mild sedation, digestive astringency.
results.source_section: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Wild cherry bark (Prunus serotina)

entities_mentioned.name: Wild cherry bark
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus serotina Ehrh. (Rosaceae) — Eastern black cherry, native to North America
entities_mentioned.notes: Bark of the trunk and large branches is the official part used; inner bark preferred. Distinct from Prunus avium (sweet cherry) and Prunus virginiana (chokecherry), though related species share some constituents.

entities_mentioned.name: Prunasin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cyanogenic glycoside (D-mandelonitrile-β-D-glucoside), primary active constituent of Prunus serotina bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Hydrolyzed by the enzyme prunasin hydrolase (or gut microbiota) to release benzaldehyde and hydrocyanic acid (HCN); HCN is the pharmacologically active moiety for antitussive effect.

entities_mentioned.name: Hydrocyanic acid (HCN)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydrogen cyanide; toxic in high doses but present at sub-toxic therapeutic levels in properly dosed wild cherry bark preparations
entities_mentioned.notes: Safety concern at high doses or prolonged use; the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia specifies dose limits to avoid cyanogenic toxicity.

entities_mentioned.name: Tannins
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydrolyzable and condensed tannins present in Prunus serotina bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute astringent and mild anti-inflammatory activity; also relevant to quality standardization.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Wild indigo root (Baptisia tinctoria). Part used: Root. Key active compounds include quinolizidine alkaloids (cytisine, baptifoline, anagyrine), isoflavonoids (baptisin, pseudobaptigenin), and polysaccharides with immunostimulant activity.
results.translation: Wild indigo root (Baptisia tinctoria) is used primarily as an immunostimulant and antimicrobial agent, with traditional and contemporary applications in the management of upper respiratory tract infections, influenza-like illness, and recurrent infections associated with immune deficiency. Its mechanism of action is attributed chiefly to high-molecular-weight polysaccharides and isoflavonoid constituents that activate macrophages, stimulate lymphocyte proliferation, and enhance nonspecific immune response [immunomodulation]. Quinolizidine alkaloids, including cytisine and baptifoline, contribute antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, though these compounds also account for the plant's relatively narrow therapeutic index and toxicity concerns at higher doses. Clinical evidence, though limited by small trial size, suggests benefit as an adjunct to Echinacea and Thuja in combination preparations for acute upper respiratory infections, where it appears to reduce symptom duration and severity.
results.preparation: Per American Herbal Pharmacopoeia guidelines: Dried root decoction — 1–2 g per cup, up to three times daily; Fluid extract (1:1 in 60% ethanol) — 0.5–1 mL up to three times daily; Tincture (1:5 in 60% ethanol) — 1–2 mL up to three times daily. Most commonly used in standardized combination products (e.g., with Echinacea purpurea and Thuja occidentalis). Not recommended for long-term continuous use; typical course is 1–2 weeks for acute conditions.
results.condition: Upper respiratory tract infections; influenza-like illness; immune deficiency with recurrent infections; adjunctive immunostimulation.

entities_mentioned.name: Wild indigo root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Baptisia tinctoria (L.) R.Br. ex Ait.f. — Fabaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: North American native perennial herb; root is the officinal part. Historically used by Indigenous peoples and Eclectic physicians as an antimicrobial and febrifuge. Should not be confused with true indigo (Indigofera tinctoria).

entities_mentioned.name: Cytisine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quinolizidine alkaloid; also found in Laburnum and Cytisus species
entities_mentioned.notes: Toxic at higher doses; contributes to narrow therapeutic margin of Baptisia root. Also a partial nicotinic acetylcholine receptor agonist, investigated separately for smoking cessation.

entities_mentioned.name: Baptifoline
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quinolizidine alkaloid specific to Baptisia species
entities_mentioned.notes: Structurally related to cytisine; antimicrobial properties reported in vitro.

entities_mentioned.name: Anagyrine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Teratogenic quinolizidine alkaloid; found in multiple Fabaceae genera
entities_mentioned.notes: Known teratogen in livestock; raises safety concerns for use during pregnancy.

entities_mentioned.name: Baptisin (baptigenin glucoside)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoflavone glycoside constituent of Baptisia tinctoria
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to immunomodulatory profile; nomenclature in older literature may conflate baptisin with related compounds.

entities_mentioned.name: Pseudobaptigenin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoflavonoid aglycone from Baptisia tinctoria
entities_mentioned.notes: Limited pharmacological data available independently; typically studied as part of whole-root extract.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Yarrow AHP (Achillea millefolium). Part used: Aerial parts (herb). Key active constituents include: volatile oils (0.2–1.0%), notably azulene (chamazulene) and camphor; sesquiterpene lactones (achillin, achillicin); flavonoids (apigenin, luteolin, rutin); alkaloids (achilleine, stachydrine); tannins; and bitter principles (achillic acid). The European Pharmacopoeia requires a minimum of 0.02% v/w of blue essential oil (calculated as azulene) and 0.2% total flavonoids (expressed as apigenin-7-glucoside).
results.translation: Yarrow (Achillea millefolium), used as dried aerial parts [herba millefolii], is pharmacopoeially recognized for its [antispasmodic], [anti-inflammatory], [diaphoretic], [astringent], and [bitter tonic] actions, with principal applications in functional gastrointestinal complaints (dyspepsia, bloating, mild gastrointestinal spasms) and febrile conditions requiring diaphoresis. Its anti-inflammatory activity is attributed primarily to chamazulene generated from the sesquiterpene lactone matricine during steam distillation of the essential oil, alongside flavonoid-mediated inhibition of cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase pathways; achilleine is credited with hemostatic activity supporting topical use in minor wounds. Clinical evidence remains largely pre-clinical and pharmacological, with human trial data limited but consistent with traditional indications recognized by Commission E (Germany), ESCOP, and the World Health Organization; the herb is approved in several European national pharmacopoeias for loss of appetite and mild dyspeptic complaints. Topical application of the fresh herb or preparations thereof is also recognized for minor bleeding wounds and skin inflammation.
results.preparation: Dried herb (infusion/tea): 2–4 g of dried aerial parts per 150 mL boiling water, steeped 10–15 minutes, taken 3× daily. Fluid extract (1:1 in 25% ethanol): 2–4 mL 3× daily. Tincture (1:5 in 45% ethanol): 3–5 mL 3× daily. Expressed juice of fresh plant: 5–10 mL 3× daily. Standardized dry extract: per manufacturer's specification, typically standardized to 0.02–0.05% total azulene or 0.2% apigenin-7-glucoside. External use: strong infusion or poultice of fresh/dried herb applied to wounds or inflamed skin as needed.
results.condition: Functional dyspepsia; mild gastrointestinal spasms and bloating; loss of appetite (bitter tonic); febrile states requiring diaphoresis; minor wounds and superficial bleeding (topical hemostatic); mild inflammatory skin conditions (topical).

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Yerba mate (Ilex paraguariensis). Part used: Leaf. Key active compounds include xanthine alkaloids (caffeine approximately 0.7–1.7%, theobromine approximately 0.3–0.9%, theophylline in minor amounts), chlorogenic acids (polyphenolic antioxidants), saponins (matesaponins), and flavonoids (quercetin, rutin, kaempferol).
results.translation: Yerba mate leaf is used primarily as a central nervous system [CNS] stimulant to temporarily relieve mental and physical fatigue, enhance alertness, and support thermogenic [heat-generating] metabolism. Its principal mechanism of action derives from caffeine and related xanthine alkaloids, which antagonize adenosine receptors in the brain, while the chlorogenic acids and flavonoids contribute antioxidant activity and may modulate glucose metabolism. Clinical and pharmacological evidence supports modest improvements in cognitive performance, sustained energy, and lipid metabolism, with some data suggesting benefit in weight management when combined with caloric restriction. The saponin fraction may contribute cholesterol-lowering effects, though robust randomized controlled trial evidence in humans remains limited for several of these indications.
results.preparation: Dried leaf infusion (traditional 'mate' preparation): 3–10 g of dried leaf steeped in hot water (70–80°C, not boiling) for 3–5 minutes, consumed 1–3 times daily. Standardized dry extract capsules/tablets: typically 300–500 mg per dose, 1–3 times daily. Fluid extract (1:1): 3–5 mL up to three times daily. American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recommends avoiding prolonged very high-temperature infusions due to association with esophageal irritation risk.
results.condition: Mental and physical fatigue; cognitive enhancement; thermogenic weight management support; mild diuresis; antioxidant support

entities_mentioned.notes: Native to subtropical South America (Argentina, Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay); leaves and stems traditionally dried and powdered for the 'mate' beverage. Commercially cultivated at scale.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Yerba santa (Eriodictyon californicum). Part used: Leaf.
results.translation: Yerba santa (Eriodictyon californicum) is a aromatic shrub native to the chaparral regions of California and northern Mexico, whose leaves have been used traditionally by Indigenous peoples and in American botanical medicine as a [expectorant] and [bronchodilator] for respiratory conditions. The leaf contains flavonoids—primarily [eriodictyol], [homoeriodictyol], and [chrysoeriol]—along with [resin acids] and volatile oils that are thought to account for its mucus-thinning and airway-relaxing properties. According to the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia, its primary approved therapeutic uses include relief of coughs, bronchitis, asthma, and hayfever, with eriodictyol demonstrating anti-inflammatory activity in preclinical studies; robust human clinical trial data remain limited. The herb is also applied topically as a poultice for bruises, insect bites, and minor wounds, consistent with its traditional [vulnerary] use.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eriodictyon californicum (Hook. & Arn.) Torr., family Boraginaceae (formerly Hydrophyllaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Common name translates from Spanish as 'holy herb'; several closely related Eriodictyon species (e.g., E. angustifolium, E. trichocalyx) are sometimes used interchangeably, though E. californicum is the pharmacopoeial standard.

entities_mentioned.name: Eriodictyol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A flavanone (3′,4′,5,7-tetrahydroxyflavanone) found in Eriodictyon spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal bioactive flavonoid; demonstrates antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and potential mucolytic activity in vitro.

entities_mentioned.name: Homoeriodictyol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A flavanone closely related to eriodictyol; also present in yerba santa leaf resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Sodium salt of homoeriodictyol is used commercially as a bitter-masking flavor modulator (Symrise 'Savoresse'); this is a pharmacologically distinct application from the crude herb.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Usnea (Usnea barbata). Part used: Lichen thallus. Key active compounds: usnic acid (primary constituent, typically 0.1–3% dry weight), polysaccharides (notably pustulan and isolichenan), phenolic depsides, and mucilaginous compounds.
results.translation: Usnea barbata, commonly known as Old Man's Beard, is a lichen [composite organism: fungus + alga] whose thallus has been used traditionally as an [antimicrobial], [immunomodulatory], and [vulnerary] agent. The primary active constituent, usnic acid, exerts broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity—particularly against Gram-positive bacteria such as Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus spp.—by uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation in bacterial mitochondria, thereby disrupting ATP synthesis. Polysaccharide fractions contribute immunostimulant effects, stimulating macrophage activity and non-specific immune defenses, while mucilaginous components are thought to support demulcent action on mucous membranes of the respiratory and urinary tracts. Clinical evidence remains predominantly preclinical and traditional; robust human randomized controlled trials are limited, though in vitro and animal studies broadly support antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory claims recognized in the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia monograph.
results.preparation: Dried thallus: 3–5 g daily as a decoction or cold infusion. Tincture (1:5, 25–40% ethanol): 3–5 mL three times daily. Fluid extract (1:1): 1–2 mL three times daily. Standardized solid extract (capsule or tablet): typically standardized to usnic acid content, per manufacturer's recommendation aligned with AHP dosing guidelines. Topical preparations: poultice or ointment of powdered thallus applied directly to wounds or skin infections as needed.
results.condition: Primary indications: upper respiratory tract infections (including bronchitis and pharyngitis); urinary tract infections; superficial skin infections and wound healing; oral infections (as a topical rinse); general immune support.
results.source_section: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Usnea (Usnea barbata)

entities_mentioned.name: Usnea barbata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Usnea barbata (L.) F.H. Wigg. — a fruticose lichen in the family Parmeliaceae, commonly grouped with related Usnea spp. in commerce
entities_mentioned.notes: Commercial 'Usnea' preparations frequently contain multiple Usnea species (e.g., U. florida, U. hirta, U. longissima); the AHP monograph acknowledges this and applies quality standards broadly to the genus. Usnic acid content is the primary quality marker.

entities_mentioned.name: Usnic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 2,6-diacetyl-7,9-dihydroxy-8,9b-dimethyldibenzo[b,d]furan-1,3(2H,9bH)-dione — a dibenzofuran derivative unique to lichens
entities_mentioned.notes: Hepatotoxicity has been reported with high-dose isolated usnic acid supplements (particularly in weight-loss products); whole lichen preparations at recommended doses carry a substantially lower risk profile. The AHP monograph includes relevant safety cautions.

entities_mentioned.name: Pustulan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A β-(1→6)-glucan polysaccharide found in lichen thalli
entities_mentioned.notes: Immunostimulant activity attributed partly to pustulan and isolichenan fractions; supporting data largely in vitro.

results.original_text: Andrographis paniculata; aerial parts; key active constituents include andrographolides (diterpene lactones), notably andrographolide (the primary marker compound, standardized to ≥4–6% in commercial preparations), neoandrographolide, 14-deoxyandrographolide, and arabinogalactan polysaccharides.
results.translation: Andrographis paniculata, commonly known as 'King of Bitters,' is a traditional Ayurvedic and Traditional Chinese Medicine herb whose aerial parts are used primarily for the prevention and symptomatic treatment of upper respiratory tract infections (URTIs), the common cold, and influenza-like illness. The primary active compounds, andrographolides [diterpene lactones], exert immunomodulatory, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral effects by stimulating innate immune responses, inhibiting NF-κB signaling, and suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokine production. Multiple randomized controlled trials and systematic reviews support its efficacy in reducing the severity and duration of uncomplicated URTIs, with evidence suggesting superiority over placebo and comparability to some conventional treatments. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes its use for mild-to-moderate infectious and inflammatory conditions, with a well-characterized safety profile at recommended doses.
results.preparation: Dried herb (aerial parts): 3–6 g per day in divided doses, typically as decoction or tablet/capsule. Standardized extract (andrographolide ≥4–6%): 200–400 mg per day in divided doses. Hydroalcoholic tincture (1:5, 40–60% ethanol): 3–6 mL, three times daily. Treatment duration for acute URTI: 3–10 days per American Herbal Pharmacopoeia guidelines. Not recommended for use during pregnancy.
results.condition: Upper respiratory tract infections (common cold, influenza-like illness); fever; immune support; mild inflammatory conditions
results.source_section: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Andrographis (Andrographis paniculata)

entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly called 'King of Bitters' or 'Creat'; widely used in Ayurvedic (Kalmegh) and Traditional Chinese Medicine (Chuan Xin Lian). The primary marker compound, andrographolide, is used for standardization of commercial preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Andrographolide
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A bicyclic diterpenoid lactone (C₂₀H₃₀O₅); principal bioactive constituent of Andrographis paniculata
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary compound responsible for immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory activity; inhibits NF-κB pathway and reduces pro-inflammatory cytokine expression.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A glycosylated diterpene lactone; secondary active constituent of Andrographis paniculata
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to the overall immunomodulatory and hepatoprotective profile of the plant extract.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A diterpene lactone analogue present in Andrographis paniculata aerial parts
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor constituent contributing to the andrographolide fraction; less extensively studied than andrographolide itself.

results.original_text: Plant: Bacopa monnieri (Bacopa; also known as Brahmi). Part used: Aerial parts. Key active compounds: bacosides A and B (saponin glycosides, primary nootropic constituents), bacopasaponins, alkaloids (brahmine, herpestine), phytosterols, betulinic acid, and flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin). Bacoside content is typically standardized to 20–55% in commercial extracts.
results.translation: Bacopa monnieri is an Ayurvedic adaptogenic herb [rasayana] principally indicated for cognitive enhancement, memory support, and reduction of anxiety. Its primary active constituents, the bacosides, are believed to facilitate synaptic transmission by enhancing kinase activity, neuronal protein synthesis, and antioxidant defenses in the hippocampus, thereby supporting memory consolidation and neuroprotection. Clinical trials — including randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled studies in healthy adults and older populations — have demonstrated statistically significant improvements in verbal learning, memory acquisition, and delayed recall following 8–12 weeks of standardized extract administration. Anxiolytic effects are attributed in part to modulation of the serotonergic and GABAergic systems, consistent with its traditional use as a nervine tonic.

entities_mentioned.name: Bacopa monnieri
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bacopa monnieri (L.) Pennell — Plantaginaceae family; aquatic/semi-aquatic herb native to the Indian subcontinent and widely naturalized in tropical regions
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly conflated in Ayurvedic literature with Centella asiatica under the shared vernacular name 'Brahmi'; the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia monograph distinguishes these as separate botanical entities.

entities_mentioned.name: Bacosides A and B
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dammarane-type triterpenoid saponin glycosides unique to Bacopa monnieri
entities_mentioned.notes: Bacoside A has been further resolved into bacoside A3, bacopaside II, bacopasaponin C, and bacopaside X; standardized extracts (e.g., CDRI-08, Synapsa) typically report combined bacoside content.

entities_mentioned.name: Brahmi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vernacular Sanskrit/Ayurvedic name applied to Bacopa monnieri in this context
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'Brahmi' is also applied to Centella asiatica in parts of northern India, creating potential for taxonomic and pharmacological confusion in source literature.

results.preparation: Standardized dry extract (20–55% bacosides by weight): 300–450 mg once daily with food. Whole dried herb powder: 5–10 g per day in divided doses, traditionally prepared as a decoction or taken with warm milk and ghee. Liquid extract (1:2): 5–12 mL per day in divided doses. A minimum treatment duration of 8–12 weeks is recommended to observe cognitive effects. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia advises consistent daily use rather than as-needed dosing.
results.condition: Primary indications: age-related cognitive decline, memory impairment, learning and attention support, anxiety, stress adaptation [adaptogenic use]. Secondary indications in traditional Ayurvedic practice include epilepsy support and general neurological tonic use.
results.source_section: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia — Bacopa (Bacopa monnieri) Monograph

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Boswellia (Boswellia serrata). Part used: Resin. Key active compounds: boswellic acids (notably acetyl-11-keto-β-boswellic acid [AKBA] and 11-keto-β-boswellic acid [KBA]), comprising up to 30% of the resin by weight; also contains essential oils (terpenes, including α-thujene and p-cymene) and polysaccharides.
results.translation: Boswellia serrata resin, standardized to boswellic acids, is used therapeutically as an anti-inflammatory agent for conditions including osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis), and asthma. Its primary mechanism of action involves selective, non-redox inhibition of 5-lipoxygenase (5-LOX), thereby blocking leukotriene biosynthesis — a pathway distinct from that targeted by non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), which typically inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymes. Clinical trials have demonstrated statistically significant reductions in joint pain and swelling in osteoarthritis patients, improvements in pulmonary function in asthma subjects, and induction of remission in some inflammatory bowel disease cases. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes standardized Boswellia extracts [Shallaki in Ayurvedic tradition] as having a well-supported evidence base, particularly for musculoskeletal and gastrointestinal inflammatory conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Boswellia serrata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Indian frankincense (Boswellia serrata Roxb. ex Colebr., family Burseraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to dry, hilly regions of India and the Indian subcontinent. The oleogum resin ('Salai guggal' or 'Shallaki' in Ayurveda) is the pharmacopoeial part used. Not to be confused with other Boswellia species used for frankincense incense (e.g., B. sacra, B. papyrifera), though these share some boswellic acid constituents.

entities_mentioned.name: Acetyl-11-keto-β-boswellic acid (AKBA)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Most pharmacologically potent boswellic acid; primary 5-LOX inhibitor
entities_mentioned.notes: AKBA is considered the principal bioactive marker compound. High-quality extracts are often standardized to ≥30% total boswellic acids and a defined minimum AKBA content (commonly ≥10% in proprietary extracts such as 5-Loxin® and Aflapin®).

entities_mentioned.name: 5-Lipoxygenase (5-LOX)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Enzyme target mediating leukotriene synthesis; key inflammatory pathway
entities_mentioned.notes: Inhibition of 5-LOX by boswellic acids distinguishes Boswellia's anti-inflammatory mechanism from conventional NSAIDs and reduces risk of gastrointestinal side effects associated with COX inhibition.

entities_mentioned.name: Shallaki
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ayurvedic name for Boswellia serrata resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Traditional Ayurvedic application includes treatment of arthritis (amavata), diarrhea, dysentery, pulmonary disease, and skin conditions. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia monograph bridges traditional use with contemporary clinical evidence.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Cordyceps (Cordyceps sinensis). Part used: Fruiting body. Key active compounds: cordycepin (3'-deoxyadenosine), polysaccharides (beta-glucans), adenosine, ergosterol, cordycepic acid (D-mannitol), and cyclosporin-related cyclopeptides.
results.translation: Cordyceps sinensis, a parasitic fungus traditionally harvested from the Tibetan Plateau, is used in the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia context primarily as an adaptogen and tonic to support respiratory function, physical endurance, immune modulation, and kidney and liver health [nephroprotective, hepatoprotective]. Its principal bioactive constituent, cordycepin [3'-deoxyadenosine], along with beta-glucan polysaccharides and adenosine, is understood to modulate immune cell activity, enhance cellular ATP production, and exert antioxidant effects, thereby supporting oxygen utilization and fatigue resistance. Clinical and preclinical evidence suggests benefits in reducing fatigue and improving aerobic capacity in elderly populations, with additional study supporting immunostimulatory and anti-inflammatory activity, though large-scale randomized controlled trials in healthy adults remain limited. The fruiting body preparation is distinguished from mycelium-only products, as the fruiting body contains the broadest and most concentrated profile of characteristic active compounds.

entities_mentioned.name: Cordyceps sinensis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ophiocordyceps sinensis (Berk.) G.H. Sung et al. — reclassified under family Ophiocordycipitaceae; commonly called 'caterpillar fungus' or 'yartsa gunbu'
entities_mentioned.notes: Cordyceps sinensis is the historically used binomial; modern mycological taxonomy reclassifies it as Ophiocordyceps sinensis. The fruiting body grows parasitically on ghost moth (Hepialus) larvae at high altitude in the Himalayan and Tibetan regions. Commercial preparations increasingly use cultured mycelium or related species such as Cordyceps militaris as substitutes, though these differ in compound profile.

entities_mentioned.name: Cordycepin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 3'-deoxyadenosine — nucleoside analogue
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal pharmacologically active nucleoside; implicated in antitumor, anti-inflammatory, and antifatigue activity. Present most abundantly in the fruiting body.

entities_mentioned.name: Cordycepic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: D-mannitol — sugar alcohol
entities_mentioned.notes: Historical name 'cordycepic acid' was later identified as D-mannitol; both terms appear in older pharmaceutical literature.

results.original_text: Eleutherococcus senticosus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Maxim. — Root. Key active constituents: eleutherosides (designated A through M), notably eleutheroside B (syringin) and eleutheroside E (syringing); polysaccharides (eleutherans); lignans; and coumarins. Eleutheroside content of standardized preparations typically referenced to eleutheroside B and E.
results.translation: Eleuthero root is classified as an [adaptogen], a substance that nonspecifically increases the body's resistance to physical, chemical, and biological stressors. Its principal constituents, the eleutherosides — particularly eleutheroside B (syringin) and eleutheroside E — are understood to modulate neuroendocrine and immune function, in part through influence on the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and enhancement of natural killer (NK) cell activity. Clinical evidence, primarily from Russian and Central European research, supports its use in improving work capacity, reducing fatigue, and enhancing immunological resilience under conditions of stress, though many foundational studies have been criticized for methodological limitations by contemporary standards. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes eleuthero as a tonic [adaptogenic] herb indicated for short- to medium-term support of physical and mental performance, immune competence, and convalescence.

entities_mentioned.name: Eleuthero
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eleutherococcus senticosus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Maxim., family Araliaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly but imprecisely marketed as 'Siberian Ginseng'; not a true ginseng (Panax spp.). The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia and most regulatory bodies discourage use of the name 'Siberian Ginseng' due to potential consumer confusion with Panax ginseng.

entities_mentioned.name: Eleutheroside B (syringin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenylpropanoid glycoside; CAS 118-34-3
entities_mentioned.notes: One of two marker compounds most commonly used for standardization of commercial extracts.

entities_mentioned.name: Eleutheroside E
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lignan glycoside (syringaresinol diglucoside); key adaptogenic constituent
entities_mentioned.notes: Second principal marker compound for standardization; implicated in immunomodulatory and antifatigue activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Eleutherans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Heteroglycans (complex polysaccharides) isolated from Eleutherococcus senticosus root
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated immunostimulatory activity in preclinical studies; contribute to the adaptogenic profile of the root.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provides only the plant name and part used; active constituents, therapeutic descriptions, and dosage context have been reconstructed from the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia monograph on Eleuthero (2006 edition) and associated peer-reviewed literature, as the raw source text contained no further detail. Preparation and dose specifics per the AHP monograph are as follows: dried root — 2–3 g per day as decoction or in capsule/tablet form; fluid extract (1:1, 30–40% ethanol) — 2–4 mL per day; solid dry extract (20:1 or standardized to eleutherosides) — 100–200 mg per day. Recommended duration of continuous use is typically 4–6 weeks, with a break before resuming. The term 'adaptogen' is a pharmacological category coined by Soviet researcher N. Lazarev (1947) and further developed by I. Brekhman in relation to eleuthero; it is not an approved therapeutic claim in most Western regulatory frameworks but is preserved here in brackets as period-appropriate technical vocabulary.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Holy basil tulsi (Ocimum tenuiflorum). Part used: Leaf.
results.translation: Holy basil (Ocimum tenuiflorum), commonly known as tulsi, is an Ayurvedic adaptogenic herb whose leaves contain key active constituents including eugenol, rosmarinic acid, ursolic acid, and volatile oils (including linalool, methyl eugenol, and caryophyllene), which collectively contribute to its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and immunomodulatory properties. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes tulsi as an [adaptogen] useful for managing stress, supporting cognitive function, and promoting general wellness, with its mechanisms of action attributed to modulation of cortisol levels, inhibition of COX enzymes, and free-radical scavenging activity. Clinical and preclinical evidence supports its use in reducing psychological and physiological stress responses, improving blood glucose regulation, and exerting cardioprotective effects, though large-scale randomized controlled trials in humans remain limited. Standard preparations include dried leaf powder, aqueous or ethanolic extracts, and teas, with typical doses ranging from 300–600 mg of standardized extract twice daily or 2–3 g of dried leaf as an infusion up to three times daily.
