entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated across South and Southeast Asia; considered sacred in Hindu tradition. Distinct from culinary sweet basil (Ocimum basilicum). Two principal chemotypes exist: Krishna (purple) and Rama (green).

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenylpropanoid compound; major volatile constituent of tulsi leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary marker compound; contributes antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Rosmarinic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyphenolic ester; antioxidant constituent
entities_mentioned.notes: Shared with rosemary and other Lamiaceae; contributes to antioxidant and anti-inflammatory profiles.

entities_mentioned.name: Ursolic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pentacyclic triterpenoid
entities_mentioned.notes: Associated with anti-inflammatory and hepatoprotective effects in preclinical studies.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Lion's mane mushroom (Hericium erinaceus). Part used: Fruiting body. Key active compounds: hericenones (aromatic compounds from fruiting body), erinacines (diterpenes from mycelium), beta-glucan polysaccharides (reported at approximately 20–40% of dry weight in quality preparations), and minor sterols including ergosterol.
results.translation: Lion's mane mushroom (Hericium erinaceus) is recognized in the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia primarily for its [nootropic] and neuroprotective properties, with principal therapeutic applications in cognitive support, mild cognitive impairment, and digestive health. Its bioactive compounds — notably hericenones and erinacines — are documented to stimulate [Nerve Growth Factor (NGF)] synthesis, supporting neuronal maintenance, regeneration, and plasticity. Clinical evidence, while still emerging, includes randomized controlled trials (notably Mori et al., 2009) demonstrating statistically significant improvements in cognitive function scores in older adults with mild cognitive impairment compared to placebo over 16 weeks, with effects attenuating upon cessation. The fruiting body's beta-glucan polysaccharides additionally confer immunomodulatory activity, and preliminary evidence supports gastroprotective effects relevant to gastric mucosal integrity.

entities_mentioned.name: Hericium erinaceus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lion's mane mushroom — a tooth fungus in the family Hericiaceae, order Russulales
entities_mentioned.notes: Also known by synonyms including Hydnum erinaceus Bull. (basionym). Widely cultivated; fruiting body and mycelium have distinct but overlapping phytochemical profiles. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia monograph focuses on the dried fruiting body as the authenticated part used.

entities_mentioned.name: Hericenones
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aromatic compounds (e.g., hericenones C–H) isolated from the fruiting body of H. erinaceus

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Morinda (Morinda officinalis How, Rubiaceae). Part used: Root. Key active constituents include iridoid glycosides (notably asperuloside and monotropein), anthraquinones, oligosaccharides (inulin-type fructooligosaccharides), polysaccharides, and trace minerals including zinc and manganese.
results.translation: Morinda officinalis root, known in Traditional Chinese Medicine as Ba Ji Tian, is classified as a kidney [shen] yang tonic and is principally indicated for conditions attributed to kidney yang deficiency, including impotence, infertility, irregular menstruation, cold extremities, lower back pain, and urinary frequency. Its pharmacological activity is thought to derive primarily from its iridoid glycosides and fructooligosaccharides, which demonstrate adaptogenic, anti-inflammatory, and mild androgenic properties in preclinical studies, with the oligosaccharide fraction showing particular promise in supporting adrenocortical function and bone density. Clinical and experimental evidence suggests immunomodulatory effects and potential benefit in osteoporosis models, though large-scale randomized controlled trials in human populations remain limited. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia monograph supports its use consistent with traditional indications pending further clinical validation.
results.preparation: Dried root decoction [tang]: 9–15 g per day in water, simmered 30–45 minutes, consumed in divided doses. Concentrated granule extracts: dose adjusted by extraction ratio per manufacturer. Tincture (1:5, 40–50% ethanol): 3–5 mL three times daily. Traditionally combined with other kidney yang tonics such as Cuscuta chinensis and Dipsacus asper in compound formulae.
results.condition: Kidney yang deficiency; impotence and male infertility; female infertility and menstrual irregularity; low back and knee pain; osteoporosis (adjunctive); urinary frequency; fatigue and cold intolerance

results.title_inferred: Muira Puama (Potency Wood) — Bark and Root
results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Muira puama (Ptychopetalum olacoides). Part used: Bark and root. Key constituents include lupeol, beta-sitosterol, campesterol, and a volatile essential oil fraction; the bark and root contain long-chain fatty acids, esters, and a resinous complex traditionally associated with bioactivity.
results.translation: Muira puama (Ptychopetalum olacoides), commonly known as 'potency wood,' is a small Amazonian tree whose bark and root have been used traditionally in Brazilian folk medicine [etnobotânica brasileira] as an [adaptogen] and [aphrodisiac], with primary applications in supporting sexual function in both men and women and alleviating [neurasthenic] fatigue. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes its use for sexual dysfunction — particularly low libido and erectile difficulties — as well as for nervous system support in cases of general debility and stress-related fatigue. Mechanistically, preliminary pharmacological research suggests central nervous system [tonic] activity, possible effects on testosterone pathways, and inhibition of [MAO] activity, though robust human clinical trial data remain limited; the most frequently cited clinical evidence includes a small French open study (Waynberg, 1994) reporting improved libido and sexual function in men using a standardized hydroalcoholic extract. The herb is considered safe at recommended doses, with no established serious adverse effects, though standardization of commercial preparations varies considerably.
results.preparation: Decoction of bark or root: 1–2 g of dried bark or root per cup of water, simmered 10–15 minutes, taken 1–3 times daily. Hydroalcoholic tincture (1:5, 70% ethanol): 4–6 mL taken 2–3 times daily. Standardized dry extract (standardized to lupeol content where applicable): typically 250–500 mg per dose, 1–2 times daily. Per American Herbal Pharmacopoeia guidelines, the hydroalcoholic extract is the preferred and best-studied form; aqueous preparations (teas/decoctions) may have reduced bioavailability of lipophilic constituents.
results.condition: Primary indications: sexual dysfunction (low libido, erectile dysfunction); neurasthenic fatigue; general nervous system debility; stress-related exhaustion.

entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'muira puama' or 'marapuama' may colloquially refer to related species; AHP monograph focuses on P. olacoides as the primary botanical source.

results.translator_notes: The AHP monograph for muira puama acknowledges a meaningful gap between traditional ethnobotanical use and the quantity of rigorous clinical evidence; translators should note that 'aphrodisiac' and 'tonic' are folk/traditional categories that do not constitute approved health claims in most regulatory jurisdictions. The lupeol standardization figure varies by source and is not universally adopted; some commercial preparations are not standardized at all. The species epithet is occasionally rendered as Ptychopetalum guyanna in older literature — these are treated as synonymous or closely related in the AHP context. results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Pau darco (Tabebuia impetiginosa). Part used: Inner bark. Key active compounds include naphthoquinones (principally lapachol and beta-lapachone), anthraquinones, flavonoids (including quercetin and lapachone derivatives), and iridoids. Lapachol content of commercial bark preparations is variable but typically cited in the range of trace to ~4% depending on species, origin, and processing.
results.translation: Pau d'arco inner bark [cortex Tabebuiae impetiginosae] is employed in traditional South American herbalism and in contemporary phytotherapy principally as an antimicrobial, antifungal, and immunomodulatory agent. Its principal bioactive constituents, the naphthoquinones lapachol and beta-lapachone, have demonstrated in vitro activity against a broad spectrum of fungi, bacteria, and certain parasites, and have been investigated for antiproliferative effects against tumor cell lines, though robust clinical trial data in human populations remain limited. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes its use as a supportive agent in infections — particularly candidiasis and other fungal conditions — and notes anti-inflammatory properties consistent with flavonoid and quinone content. Clinical evidence is largely preliminary; practitioners should be aware that lapachol at high isolate doses carries potential toxicity risks (anticoagulant effects, gastrointestinal disturbance), though whole-bark preparations at recommended doses are generally considered better tolerated.

entities_mentioned.name: Pau darco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tabebuia impetiginosa (Mart. ex DC.) Standl. (syn. Handroanthus impetiginosus), family Bignoniaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Also marketed as lapacho or taheebo. Taxonomy has been revised; many sources now place the species in genus Handroanthus. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia monograph uses the historically dominant trade name Tabebuia impetiginosa.

entities_mentioned.name: Lapachol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 2-hydroxy-3-(3-methyl-2-butenyl)-1,4-naphthoquinone
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary marker compound used for quality standardization of pau d'arco bark. Isolated lapachol was investigated by the US National Cancer Institute in the 1970s but abandoned due to toxicity at therapeutic doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Beta-lapachone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 3,4-dihydro-2,2-dimethyl-2H-naphthol[1,2-b]pyran-5,6-dione
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-occurring naphthoquinone; subject of ongoing oncology and infectious disease research.

entities_mentioned.name: Quercetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 3,3′,4′,5,7-pentahydroxyflavone
entities_mentioned.notes: Ubiquitous flavonoid contributing to anti-inflammatory activity profile.

results.preparation: Per American Herbal Pharmacopoeia guidance: (1) Decoction — simmer 1–2 teaspoons (approx. 2–4 g) of dried inner bark in 250–500 mL water for 15–20 minutes; drink 2–3 cups daily. (2) Standardized dry extract (capsule/tablet) — typically 200–300 mg standardized to lapachol content, 2–3 times daily. (3) Tincture (1:5 in 25% ethanol) — 2–4 mL three times daily. (4) Topical preparations for fungal skin conditions as directed. Duration of use is generally limited to 4–8 weeks without practitioner supervision.
results.condition: Primary indications: fungal infections (candidiasis, dermatophytosis), bacterial infections (supportive), immune support, anti-inflammatory conditions; investigational use in oncology support.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Pygeum (Prunus africana). Part used: Bark. Key active compounds include phytosterols (notably beta-sitosterol), pentacyclic triterpenes (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid), ferulic acid esters (n-docosanol, tetracosanol), and fatty acids.
results.translation: Pygeum bark [Prunus africana] is primarily used in the management of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), where it is indicated for reducing lower urinary tract symptoms such as nocturia, urinary frequency, and weak urinary flow. Its mechanism of action is multifactorial: phytosterols reduce prostatic inflammation by inhibiting prostaglandin biosynthesis, ferulic acid esters lower prolactin-stimulated uptake of testosterone in prostatic tissue, and pentacyclic triterpenes exert anti-edematous effects on the prostate. Clinical evidence from multiple randomized, placebo-controlled trials and meta-analyses supports modest but statistically significant improvements in urinary symptom scores, peak urinary flow rate, and nocturia compared to placebo. The American Herbal Pharmacopoeia recognizes standardized lipophilic bark extract as the primary therapeutic preparation, with quality defined by minimum content of total sterols and triterpenic acids.

entities_mentioned.name: Pygeum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus africana (Hook.f.) Kalkman; syn. Pygeum africanum Hook.f. — African cherry, a large evergreen tree native to sub-Saharan Africa and highland Madagascar
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed on CITES Appendix II due to overexploitation; bark harvesting is regulated internationally. Formerly classified in the genus Pygeum, now placed in Prunus (Rosaceae).

entities_mentioned.name: beta-sitosterol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A phytosterol (plant sterol) found widely in plant kingdom; one of the principal bioactive constituents of Pygeum bark extract
entities_mentioned.notes: Also the active constituent in saw palmetto and other BPH botanicals; inhibits 5-alpha-reductase and prostaglandin synthesis.

entities_mentioned.name: ferulic acid esters (n-docosanol, tetracosanol)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Long-chain fatty alcohol esters of ferulic acid; lipophilic constituents specific to Pygeum bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Believed to reduce prolactin-mediated androgen uptake in prostatic tissue, distinguishing Pygeum's mechanism from other BPH botanicals.

entities_mentioned.name: ursolic acid / oleanolic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pentacyclic triterpene acids; anti-inflammatory and anti-edematous secondary metabolites
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute anti-inflammatory effects relevant to prostatic swelling and urinary obstruction.

results.original_text: Source: American Herbal Pharmacopoeia. Plant: Sarsaparilla (Smilax ornata). Part used: Root. Key active compounds: steroidal saponins (sarsasaponin, smilasaponin, sarsaparilloside; collectively 1–3% by dry weight), phytosterols (sitosterol, stigmasterol), flavonoids, and resin.
results.translation: Sarsaparilla root [Smilax ornata] has been used traditionally as a [depurative] (blood purifier), anti-inflammatory, and tonic, with primary applications in the management of skin disorders (psoriasis, eczema), rheumatic and arthritic conditions, and as a supportive agent in urinary tract health. Its steroidal saponins are thought to bind endotoxins in the gut, reducing systemic inflammatory load, and may exhibit mild androgenic or adaptogenic activity, though these mechanisms remain incompletely characterized in clinical literature. According to the American Herbal Pharmacopoeia, clinical evidence for efficacy is largely drawn from traditional use and limited in vitro or animal studies; robust controlled human trials are sparse, and therapeutic claims should be interpreted with appropriate caution.
results.preparation: Dried root: 2–4 g as a decoction (simmered 10–15 minutes in water), taken up to three times daily. Fluid extract (1:1 in 20% ethanol): 8–15 mL daily in divided doses. Tincture (1:5 in 40% ethanol): 15–60 mL daily in divided doses. Standardized extracts should conform to American Herbal Pharmacopoeia identity and purity specifications for Smilax ornata root.
results.condition: Skin disorders (psoriasis, eczema, dermatitis); rheumatic and arthritic complaints; urinary tract support; traditional use as a systemic [depurative] and tonic.
